## [00:00 - 03:06]

Hi, this is Don Crowther. Let's talk about common webinar registration page errors. So I'm going to tell you the nine mistakes that people make all the time. All right, so here they are. Number one, it's not designed for mobile. 50, 60, sometimes upwards of 70% of all the traffic you're going to have coming to your webinar registration page is going to be on a mobile phone. And so you have to make sure it looks good on mobile, that the key facts are right there on that first page. And the signup link is above the fold. Now, I'm going to use this term above the fold several times here. So let me just describe it if you can case you don't know it above the fold means you don't have to scroll to see it. And so it needs to be there on that first page on your mobile phone before you scroll. It also has to load quickly on mobile. Now most website, web page development programs have the option now where you can click to see it on mobile. And then you can make editing changes to that that are saved just for that for a mobile phone. And so basically what happens getting technical on you for a second is when someone arrives on a page the program asks, what kind of device are they looking at this on if it's mobile, show them this version of the page of his desktop show them this version of the page. And by the way, there's a tablet piece in between. You're going to want to make sure that you've designed it. Frankly, I designed it first on mobile, I make it look good on mobile, then I convert that into something that looks good on desktop, not the other way around because that's the way that it's prioritized today in the viewership that's coming to it. And so I like to design first where my viewers are going to be coming from. Now very related to this, but for other devices is you want to make sure that your sign up button is not below the fold. And so to get there, shorten your copy, move that sign up button up so that it is above the fold, people don't have to scroll to be able to see it. Next mistake, the sign up button disappears into the design of the page. This usually happens because people want to want their page to look pretty. They may even have like an artistic background or have a designer who's doing it. And artistic people hate things that don't look good. Well, here's the problem. When your button merges into the look or disappears into the design of the page, then no one clicks on it. So you want to use a bold color and design, you want that button to stand out. And I personally like to have a button color that isn't used anywhere else on the page. So I use when I work with a designer, I use one word
